rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions (the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking); ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank and the published set is a bimodal artifact, not a representative distribution. Georgia does not currently appear in the published index at all and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt — see rank_note). Calibrated to the methodology's dimension anchors first, then sanity-checked against named published peers (Connecticut 83.0, Minnesota 84.4, Massachusetts 94.4 far above; Idaho/Indiana/Missouri/Dakotas 25.0 cluster; Tennessee 23.4, Louisiana 21.9, Florida 20.3, Alabama/Arkansas/Mississippi 12.5) and against the other five states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9 (superseded, corrected 53.8), Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Georgia at 26.9 sits marginally above the Idaho/Indiana/Missouri/Dakotas cluster and above Tennessee, Louisiana and Florida. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs: dimension mean 2.075, baseComposite 26.875, stdDev 0.2817 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returns {"composite":26.9,"band":"Developing"} — matches the target exactly.
attribution_note: Scores Georgia's own state conduct. HB 1105 (2024), which mandates statewide sheriff cooperation with ICE across all 159 counties, is a state legislative choice and is fully scored (AWR/EQU). Pathways to Coverage is a federally-approved Section 1115 waiver, but the decision to pursue a narrow work-requirement waiver instead of full Medicaid expansion is Georgia's own policy choice and is scored at EQU/BND/ACT; Georgia is one of ten states that has declined full expansion by its own legislative choice, not federal compulsion. The DOJ investigation and Eighth Amendment findings on Georgia prisons are a federal action, but Georgia's own response — the $600M corrections package, the pace and completeness of remediation, and whether findings were addressed proactively or only under federal pressure — is state conduct and is what is scored (ACT/ACC). The September 2025 Hyundai-LG plant raid was a federal immigration enforcement operation; Georgia State Patrol's participation in that operation is state conduct and is scored (SYS), while Governor Kemp's subsequent public call for federal visa-system reform is noted as commentary on federal policy and is not itself scored as a Georgia systemic fix. The LIFE Act, SB 39, SB 185, SB 202/SB 189 election law, and the RICO prosecution of Stop Cop City defendants are entirely state-originated and fully scored.
key_evidence: Georgia responds to crises it is forced to confront and not to ones it can avoid: after the DOJ found Eighth Amendment violations across 17 prisons in October 2024, Georgia committed over $600 million to corrections within about 90 days, while it has made no comparable move on health coverage, where it ranks 49th of 50 states on adult uninsurance at 12.0%.

In November 2024, after ProPublica published the state Maternal Mortality Review Committee's own conclusion that Amber Thurman's death was likely preventable, Georgia dismissed all 32 committee members; as of March 2025 the state would not disclose who replaced them.

Beginning with the March 2025 report, the Georgia Department of Corrections stopped publishing manner of death; in 2025 it reported 301 deaths but identified only 295, leaving six people who died in state custody with no name, facility or cause ever disclosed.

HB 1013, signed 2022-04-04, is Georgia's strongest documented reform: it requires insurers to cover mental health and substance-use care on par with physical health and created a public complaint repository with annual reporting, and has continued through low-visibility administrative channels with no publicity benefit.

Georgia signed a $2 billion income and property tax relief package on 2026-03-03 in the same budget cycle that its child welfare agency (DFCS) faced a projected $85.7 million shortfall and a $15.3 million pilot for students in poverty was cut for FY2027.

evidence.claim: state has chosen for over a decade not to close a measured coverage gap

Over 30% of Georgia's 159 counties are classified as maternity care deserts—counties without birthing hospitals or obstetrical providers.

The Georgia State Financing and Investment Commission approved redirecting $100 million to the Georgia Development Authority for disaster relief financial support for farmers and debris cleanup for timber producers impacted by Hurricane Helene.

The program enrolled only 3,500 individuals in its first year, far short of the state's projected goal of 25,028.
Effective October 1, 2025, the approved extension of the Georgia Pathways to Coverage program will go into effect.
Legislators approved $434 million in new funding for the Georgia Department of Corrections for the current fiscal year and about $200 million in new spending for the Fiscal Year 2026 budget.
$15.3 million was eliminated for FY 2027, which previously funded a one-time pilot for an 'opportunity weight' designed to increase support for students in poverty.

The Kemp recommendations speak directly to some of the DOJ's concerns—particularly staffing and facility conditions—but not others, including sexual safety and the management of gang members.

Georgia is among 10 states that have not expanded Medicaid eligibility.

Georgia's child welfare system remains shaken after projected $85.7 million budget shortfall.

Includes measures to hire more correctional and security officers, improve their pay and update their training.

Agencies submit annual reports to the Georgia Data Analytics Center, which must prepare a unified public report.

slug_note: Slug is georgia-us-states, not georgia. The US state collides with Georgia the country (countries.json), which owns entity-records/georgia.json. The display name Georgia is correct and unchanged; only the slug is disambiguated. See research/LEGACY_INDEX_DEFECTS_2026-07-19.md.
